coredata.dc:title: Ethanol in aqueous hydrogen peroxide solution: Hydrothermal synthesis of highly photoluminescent carbon dots as multifunctional nanosensors
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Yaoping
Yang, Jing
Jia, Li
Yu, Jun-Sheng
coredata.dc:description: A novel synthetic strategy has been developed for facile, green and low-cost fabrication of highly photoluminescent carbon dots (C-dots) by hydrothermal treatment of ethanol in aqueous hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) solution. Noticeably, the synthesized C-dots present an unexpectedly large quantum yield of 38.7% without any post-treatments. In contrast to the most amorphous C-dots, the ethanol-derived C-dots possess an essentially crystalline nature as evidenced by the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy and selected-area electron diffraction. It is found that the C-dots can serve as multifunctional fluorescence nanosensors to detect pH, temperature, and the concentration of hypochlorite ion (ClO−). The PL intensity of C-dots decreases dramatically as pH increases from 3 to 11. Based on this feature, a C-dots coated fluorescent paper for visual detection of pH by naked eyes has been successfully prepared. The C-dots reveals a linear and reversible PL response toward the temperature in the range of 10–80°C, suggesting the great potential for design of temperature-sensitive devices. The selective quantification of ClO− concentration from 0.1 to 10μM with a detection limit as low as 0.08μM is achieved by ClO−-induced PL quenching of C-dots. Moreover, the C-dots applied for ClO− assay in real water samples with satisfactory recovery is demonstrated.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of H2O2 concentration, reaction temperature, and time on synthesis of C-dots and a proposed formation mechanism
C-dots as pH and temperature nanosensors
Sensitive and selective detection of ClO−

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for the synthesis of C-dots as multifunctional nanosensors. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) TEM and (b) high-resolution TEM images of the C-dots. Insets in (a) and (b): size distribution and SAED pattern of the C-dots.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectrum of the C-dots.
(a) XPS survey, (b) C1s, (c) O1s, (d) FTIR, (e) 13C NMR, and (f) 1H NMR spectra of the C-dots. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum and (b) PL spectra of the C-dots. Inset in (a): enlarged UV–vis absorption spectrum in the region of 350–600nm. Inset in (b): normalized PL spectra of the C-dots. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) PL spectra of the C-dots dispersed in solutions with different pH values (from 3 to 11). Inset in (a): PL intensity at the peak position of the C-dots dispersed in solutions with different pH values. (b) Photographs of filter paper without (upper left) and with (lower left) C-dots coating, and (right) the C-dots coated paper for pH detection. The pH values of the solutions dripped on the paper are 3, 7 and 11 (from up to down). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) PL spectra of the C-dots solution at different temperatures (10–80°C). Inset in (a): PL intensity at the peak position of the C-dots solution at different temperatures. (b) The reversible temperature-dependence of PL intensity over the range of 10–80°C. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) PL spectra of the C-dots solution upon addition of various concentrations of ClO− in a pH 7.0 solution (from up to down: 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 15μM). (b) The relationship between (I 0 − I)/I 0 and concentration of ClO− from 0 to 15μM. Inset in (b): a linear region as the concentration of ClO− ranges from 0.1 to 10μM. (c) Selectivity of the C-dots toward ClO−. The concentration of ClO− is 10μM. The concentrations of other ions are 1mM, except that the concentrations of Ag+ and Hg2+ are 100μM. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Detection of ClO− in tap water.

head.ce:title: Ethanol in aqueous hydrogen peroxide solution: Hydrothermal synthesis of highly photoluminescent carbon dots as multifunctional nanosensors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210093, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing 210029, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel synthetic strategy has been developed for facile, green and low-cost fabrication of highly photoluminescent carbon dots (C-dots) by hydrothermal treatment of ethanol in aqueous hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) solution. Noticeably, the synthesized C-dots present an unexpectedly large quantum yield of 38.7% without any post-treatments. In contrast to the most amorphous C-dots, the ethanol-derived C-dots possess an essentially crystalline nature as evidenced by the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy and selected-area electron diffraction. It is found that the C-dots can serve as multifunctional fluorescence nanosensors to detect pH, temperature, and the concentration of hypochlorite ion (ClO−). The PL intensity of C-dots decreases dramatically as pH increases from 3 to 11. Based on this feature, a C-dots coated fluorescent paper for visual detection of pH by naked eyes has been successfully prepared. The C-dots reveals a linear and reversible PL response toward the temperature in the range of 10–80°C, suggesting the great potential for design of temperature-sensitive devices. The selective quantification of ClO− concentration from 0.1 to 10μM with a detection limit as low as 0.08μM is achieved by ClO−-induced PL quenching of C-dots. Moreover, the C-dots applied for ClO− assay in real water samples with satisfactory recovery is demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:para: With unique optical properties, low environmental hazard, excellent biocompatibility, and robust chemical inertness [1,2], carbon dots (C-dots) are showing promise in a myriad of applications, such as bioimaging [3,4], drug delivery [5], sensors [6–11], optoelectronics [12], and photocatalysis [13,14]. Owning to the significant progress in synthetic strategies, numerous routes including arc discharge [15], laser ablation [16], plasma treatment [17], electrochemical synthesis [18,19], ultrasonic/microwave approach [20,21], acidic/thermal oxidation [22–24], and hydrothermal technique [25–29], have been explored to fabricate C-dots from a variety of carbon precursors. However, most of these routes suffer from expensive equipments, harsh synthetic conditions, and tedious processes, leading to manufacturing difficulties and high costs for large scale production. Moreover, the resultant C-dots usually show low photoluminescence (PL) quantum yields (QYs), typically less than 10%, which impedes their practical applications. Surface passivation and heteroatoms doping are two effective ways to enhance the QYs of C-dots [16,21,30]. Nevertheless, surface passivation needs complex post-treatment steps, and heteroatoms doping often require special nitrogen-containing organic precursors which are toxic and difficult to obtain. Currently, facile and low-cost synthesis of highly photoluminescent C-dots (QYs>30%) without additional surface passivation or heteroatoms doping is hardly achieved despite great efforts have been made.

Ethanol is one of the most common reagents in laboratory. It has been employed as a cheap and abundant carbon source for preparation of carbon nanotubes by catalytic chemical vapor deposition [31]. The production of graphene from ethanol based on solvothermal treatment and sonication has also been reported [32]. However, the synthesis of ethanol-derived C-dots is seriously limited [33], as much attention has focused on fabricating C-dots from graphene-based materials [13–16,18,19], saccharides [12,20], citric acid [21,23,25], and biomasses [8,17,26–28]. In the present work, we develop a novel one-step approach to synthesize highly fluorescent C-dots by hydrothermal oxidation of ethanol with an environmentally friendly oxidant, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Compared with other methods, our approach is more economic and much greener, because it only requires the most affordable and benign reagents, ethanol and H2O2, and no expensive equipments, severe synthetic conditions, or complicated procedures are demanded. The resulting C-dots are highly crystalline with abundant oxygenous functional groups. Their QY has been significantly improved to be 38.7% without further surface passivation or heteroatoms doping, much higher than the values of most reported doping-free and nitrogen-doped C-dots. It is remarkable that the C-dots can act as multifunctional fluorescence nanosensors to detect pH, temperature, and the concentration of hypochlorite ion (ClO−) in aqueous solution (Fig. 1 ). Importantly, a portable paper-based sensor has been prepared by simply coating the C-dots on the commercial filter paper, and visual fluorescence detection of pH by naked eyes is achieved under a UV lamp. Moreover, the feasibility of C-dots for ClO− assay in local tap water is presented.

Ethanol and hydrogen peroxide (30wt%) were purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagents Factory (Nanjing, China). Sodium hypochlorite solution was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other reagents and chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Deionized water produced through a Millipore water purification system (⩾18MΩ, Milli-Q, Millipore) was used throughout the experiments.

The C-dots were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of ethanol in H2O2 solution. For a typical process, 3mL of ethanol was added into a 50mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave containing 4mL of commercial H2O2 (30wt%) and 23mL of deionized water. The final H2O2 concentration was 4wt% in a 30mL solution. Hydrothermal treatment of the above solution at 180°C for 12h resulted in a yellow dispersion of C-dots. After purifying the C-dots with a dialysis tube (molecular weight cut off: 100–500Da) and evaporating the solvent in an oven at 60°C, about 5mg of C-dots powder was obtained.

The QY of the C-dots was measured using quinine sulfate as the standard and calculated with following equation:
QY
x
=
QY
std
(
I
x
/
I
std
) (
A
std
/
A
x
) (
η
x
2
/
η
std
2
)
where the subscript “x” designates the C-dots, the subscript “std” designates quinine sulfate, “QY” stands for the quantum yield, “I” stands for the integrated PL intensity, “A” stands for the absorbance, and “η” stands for the refractive index of the solvent. Quinine sulfate (QY: 54.6%) was dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4 (refractive index: 1.33) and the C-dots were dissolved in water (refractive index: 1.33). In order to minimize reabsorption effects, absorbance value of the individual solution was kept below 0.10 at the excitation wavelength.

In a typical run, 2mg of C-dots was dispersed in a 1000mL of Na2HPO4–NaH2PO4 buffer solution (20mM, pH 7.0). Then, 1mL of a certain concentration of ClO− solution (0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 15μM) was added into 4mL of the C-dots dispersion. The PL spectra with excitation wavelength of 400nm were recorded. The sensitivity and selectivity measurements were conducted in triplicate.

For the analysis of real samples, the local tap water was freshly collected without any pretreatment. A standard addition method was applied to evaluate the validity of the proposed sensor. The procedure was the same as above, except that the solutions added into the C-dots dispersion were 0.5mL of tap water and 0.5mL of ClO− solution (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10μM).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern were taken on a JEM-2100 electron microscope operating at an accelerated voltage of 200kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded with a Shimadu XRD-6000 powder X-ray diffractometer with Cu Ká radiation (1.54056Å). The Raman spectrum of as-prepared samples was obtained on a LabRAM ARAMIS Confocal Raman Microscope with a laser at an excitation wavelength of 532nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed with a PHI 5000 VersaProbe X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectrum was collected in the KBr medium on a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were carried out with a Bruker Avance III 400 spectrometer by dispersing 20mg of C-dots in 500μL of CDCl3. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were monitored by a UV-3600 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired on a RF-5301PC spectrophotometer. Time-resolved fluorescence spectrum was measured with an Edinburgh FLS 920 spectroscope.

Hydrothermal treatment of ethanol (3mL) in the presence of aqueous H2O2 solution (4wt% in final 30mL volume) at 180°C for 12h led to a yellow dispersion of C-dots. TEM image (Fig. 2 a) shows that the C-dots are nearly spherical and well-dispersed. The average diameter of the C-dots is 4.8nm and the size distribution accords well with Gaussian distribution by measuring 300 nanoparticles (inset of Fig. 2a). High-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2b) reveals clear lattice fringes with spacing of 0.21nm, quite close to (102) lattice spacing of graphitic carbon (JCPDS 26–1076), indicating high crystallinity of the C-dots [34]. Three distinct diffuse rings corresponding to (102), (110), and (116) planes of graphitic carbon are observed in the SAED pattern (inset of Fig. 2b), which further confirms their crystalline nature. XRD pattern (Fig. 3 a) exhibits a typical broad peak at about 24°, associated with the graphitic structure [12]. A disordered D band at 1360cm−1 and a crystalline G band at 1589cm−1 are presented in the Raman spectrum (Fig. 3b), demonstrating the co-existence of sp3 and sp2 carbon clusters inside the C-dots [35].

XPS was used to investigate to the chemical composition and chemical bonding of the C-dots. XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 4 a) displays two prominent features of C1s at 284eV and O1s at 532eV with atomic percentages of 78.93% and 21.07%. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 4b) can be resolved into three peaks at 284.5, 286.3, and 288.5eV, attributed to C–C/CC, C–O, and CO groups respectively [36]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) exhibits two oxygen states of CO at 531.8eV and C–O at 532.9eV [27]. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 4d) shows the characteristic absorption bands of O–H stretching vibration at 3413cm−1, C–H stretching vibrations at 2978 and 2931cm−1, CO stretching vibration at 1728cm−1, CC stretching vibration at 1633cm−1, C–H bending vibration at 1384cm−1, and C–O stretching vibration at 1186 and 1043cm−1. The 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. 4e) presents a large proportion of sp3-hybridized C–C/C–O carbons in 0–100ppm, a small part of sp2-hybridized CC alkene/aromatic carbons in 100–160ppm, as well as a certain amount of CO carboxylic/carbonyl carbons in 170–190ppm. The aromatic protons are also detected in the 6–8ppm region of 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. 4f). The NMR results agree well with the XPS and FTIR analysis. Therefore, it is safe to conclude that the as-synthesized products are carbon-rich, oxygen-containing nanodots, which have partial disordered graphite-like structure and are functionalized with abundant oxygenous groups.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 5 a) shows that the obtained C-dots solution has three main absorption features: an intensive peak at 222nm due to the π–π∗ transition of CC bonds, a shoulder at 281nm assigned to the n–π∗ transition of CO bonds, and a weak band at 406nm originated from the functionalized surface states [37]. Fig. 5b illustrates an excitation-wavelength-dependent PL property of C-dots. The strongest PL peak is centered at 456nm for 400nm excitation. The peak position gradually red-shifts from 456 to 549nm (blue to yellowish-green) with slow decrease of intensity when the excitation wavelength is tuned from 400 to 520nm. This PL behavior probably arises from a distribution of different surface emissive trap sites [16], which is a general characteristic of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials. Using quinine sulfate (54.6% in 0.1M sulfuric acid) as a standard, the QY of the C-dots is calculated to be 38.7% at 400nm excitation, much higher than the values of most reported doping-free and nitrogen-doped C-dots. Such high QY may be associated with the highly crystalline nature of the C-dots, which effectively reduces the non-radiative electron–hole recombination centers, and renders the C-dots strongly fluorescent. The time-resolved PL decay profile (Fig. S1) can be well described by a double-exponential decay function giving a short lifetime of 3.48ns (37.85%) and a long lifetime of 5.02ns (62.15%). The long lifetime with high proportion is suggested to be related to the oxygenous groups on the surface [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of H2O2 concentration, reaction temperature, and time on synthesis of C-dots and a proposed formation mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: It is found that ethanol itself can not convert into C-dots without the assistance of H2O2, as evidenced by the featureless UV–vis absorption spectrum and negligible PL intensity of the obtained solution (Fig. S2a). When hydrothermal treatment of ethanol in the presence of 1wt% H2O2, two absorption peaks at 222 and 285nm and an absorption tail in the range of 350–500nm appear, indicating the formation of C-dots. The absorbance of these features enhance as the H2O2 concentration is regulated from 1 to 5wt%, which is suggestive of the increasing concentration of C-dots. Further raising the H2O2 concentration from 5 to 8wt%, however, leads to the decrease of absorbance. It is speculated that too high H2O2 concentration results in over-oxidation of ethanol to CO2 and H2O, reducing the yield of C-dots. Correspondingly, the PL spectra (Fig. S2b) show the same trends as those observed in UV–vis absorption monitoring. We get the maximum QY of 38.7% when fixing the H2O2 concentration at 4wt% (Fig. S2c). The reaction temperature is also important for C-dots synthesis. None of C-dots generates below 120°C. Upon changing the reaction temperature from 140 to 180°C, the optical adsorption (Fig. S3a), PL emission (Fig. S3b), and QY (Fig. S3c) of the C-dots dispersion increase sharply, demonstrating that higher temperature facilitates the carbonization of C-dots and creates a better surface structure. The time-dependent experiments (Fig. S4a–c) reveal that the nucleation of C-dots happens as the reaction proceeds for 1.5h, and the C-dots reach the highest QY at the reaction time of 12h.

On the basis of the above observations and previous studies about the formation process of carbogenic materials from small molecules, we propose a potential formation mechanism of the C-dots. In the first stage, hydrothermal oxidation of ethanol takes place in the presence of H2O2, yielding diverse products such as acetaldehyde, acetic acid, glyoxal, and glycollic acid, etc. [38,39]. Then, some oxidation products react each other to produce unsaturated aldehydes. For instance, crotonaldehyde can form by the aldol condensation of acetaldehyde under acidic condition. After that, the labile unsaturated aldehydes acting as new carbon sources [40] proceed consecutively through polymerization, aromatization, and ultimately generation and growth of carbogenic nuclei [41]. Nonetheless, the detailed reaction path of C-dots is still unclear due to the existence of various intermediates and the limit of techniques for identifying all derivable compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: C-dots as pH and temperature nanosensors
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of UV exposure, ionic strength, pH, and temperature on PL intensity of C-dots were investigated. There is no obvious decrease in PL intensity under continuous UV irradiation (Fig. S5) or at different NaCl concentrations (Fig. S6), indicating the good PL stability of C-dots. Interestingly, the C-dots possess a pH-sensitive PL feature. As illustrated in Fig. 6 a, the PL intensity of C-dots is strong in acidic solutions (pH<5), but drops rapidly as pH increases from 6 to 9, and remains at very low values under alkaline conditions (pH>10). This pH-dependent fluorescence arises from the protonation and deprotonation of carboxyl groups on the surface of C-dots [42]. It has been reported that the carboxylic acid groups coupled with nearby aromatic carbon atoms can serve as fluorophore species. In acidic solutions, the concentration of carboxylic acid groups on C-dots surface is high, meaning that a large number of fluorophore species exist, thus bringing about intensive PL emissions. When pH increases, the deprotonation of carboxyl groups is accelerated, which reduces the amount of fluorophore species and consequently weakens the PL intensity.

We have made a C-dots coated paper for visual fluorescence detection of pH. A piece of filter paper (2×3cm) was immersed into a C-dots solution (1mgmL−1) for 2h. Then, the paper was removed from the solution and placed in an oven at 40°C for 1h. Under a handheld UV-lamp, this paper emits blue fluorescence, while the raw paper without any treatment is nonluminous (Fig. 6b, left side), demonstrating the successful preparation of C-dots coated paper. 5μL of solutions with pH values of 3, 7 and 11 were dripped on three regions of the fluorescent paper and air-dried for 20min (Fig. 6b, right side). Under UV light, the region dripped with the acidic solution (pH=3) exhibits an extra-bright blue fluorescent ring, but the fluorescence of the ring becomes weak in the region dripped with the neutral solution (pH=7), and the region dripped with the alkaline solution (pH=11) is apparently black. Although the accurate pH assay using the C-dots coated paper has not been achieved, the distinct variation of fluorescence brightness on the paper can also indicate the rough pH values with naked eyes.

The temperature-dependent fluorescence of C-dots is also found. Fig. 7 a records the gradual decrease of PL intensity as the temperature increases from 10 to 80°C. There is a good linear relationship with a correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.998 between temperature and PL intensity (inset of Fig. 7a). The temperature-response of PL intensity shows an excellent reversibility during consecutive heating–cooling cycles within the temperature range of 10–80°C (Fig. 7b). It is suggested that the temperature induced PL quenching of C-dots is derived from the temperature enhanced population of non-radiative channels of surface (trap/defect) states [43–45]. At low temperature, the non-radiative channels are not thermally activated; therefore, the excited electrons can radiatively emit photons. Once the temperature is increased, the non-radiative channels become thermally activated; therefore, an increasing number of the excited electrons return to the ground state through a non-radiative process, and correspondingly the PL intensity decreases. This optically temperature-sensitive property provides great opportunities for design of C-dots based temperature-monitoring devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of ClO−
ce:section.ce:para:
In recent years, C-dots have been widely used to detect positive ions especially Hg2+, Cu2+ and Fe3+ in aqueous solutions [6–9]. However, the development of C-dots for detection of negative ions is limited. Hypochlorite (ClO−) is a powerful oxidant which functions as an antimicrobial agent in water treatment and living organisms [46]. We attempted to use the C-dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for ClO− assay. Fig. 8 a shows the PL quenching of C-dots upon addition of various concentrations of ClO− solutions. The relationship between the quenching ratio (I 0 − I)/I 0 and ClO− concentration is addressed in Fig. 8b, and a nice linear correlation exists over the range of ClO− concentration from 0.1 to 10μM, giving a calibration curve of (I 0 − I)/I 0 =0.077×
C
(
ClO
-
)
(μM)−0.007 with R 2 of 0.991 (inset of Fig. 8b). The limit of detection is determined to be 0.08μM based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, lower than that of other methods [46–48]. The selectivity of C-dots for ClO− detection is evaluated. Common ions including Cl−, Br−, NO3 −, ClO4 −, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, S2−, CO3 2−, PO4 3−, Cu2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, and Cr3+ at a concentration of 1mM, and Ag+ and Hg2+ at a concentration of 100μM under the same conditions as those used with ClO− (10μM) have nearly no interference to the PL intensity (Fig. 8c), indicating the excellent selectivity of the C-dots sensing system toward ClO−. It is believed that the PL response of C-dots to ClO− originates from the surface state change of C-dots caused by ClO− oxidation, which destroys the structure of fluorophore species on the C-dots surface [49]. Several experiments can prove this mechanism, in which the oxidants with stronger oxidation capability than ClO−, such as MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2−, can also quench the PL of C-dots (Fig. S7), while the oxidants with weaker oxidation capability than ClO−, including Fe3+, Ag+, Br2 and I2, have nearly no effect on the PL intensity of C-dots. Fortunately, most natural water samples would not contain MnO4 − and Cr2O7 2− [49]. Thus, the interference of such strong oxidants can be ruled out.

The C-dots based fluorescent sensing platform is applied for ClO− assay in real water samples. The results are shown in Table 1 . The ClO− concentration in local tap water is found to be 4.96±0.10μM. Using a standard addition method, the sample recoveries are in the range of 96–106%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) are between 1.6% and 2.8%, both of which are suggestive of the high accuracy and credibility of the analysis.

In summary, we have developed a H2O2-assisted hydrothermal technique for synthesis of highly photoluminescent C-dots from ethanol that can serve as multifunctional nanosensors. The present work has several advantages over the previous studies. First, the carbon source used here is ethanol, which is the most common, abundant, and inexpensive regent in laboratory. Second, our synthetic method is environmentally friendly and facile, because in addition to ethanol, only a green oxidant, H2O2, is required in the experiment, and no severe synthetic conditions or complicated procedures are demanded. Third, the ethanol-derived C-dots possess a high QY of 38.7% without any surface passivation or heteroatoms doping. Fourth, the C-dots with unique PL properties exhibit an excellent and multifunctional sensing performance in determination of pH, temperature, and ClO− concentration in aqueous solution. Finally, a fluorescent paper coated with C-dots for visual detection of pH by naked eyes is successfully prepared, and the C-dots applied for ClO− assay in real water samples is achieved. This work provides a new route for green and low-cost synthesis of ultrabright C-dots from cheap precursors, and creates more opportunity for practical applications of C-dots in sensors and beyond.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Basic Research Program (2010CB732401), National Science Fund for Creative Research Groups (21121091), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (20875045).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2015.06.018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Carbon dots for optical imaging in vivo
Carbon nanodots featuring efficient FRET for real-time monitoring of drug delivery and two-photon imaging
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
Development of a carbon quantum dots-based fluorescent Cu2+ probe suitable for living cell imaging
Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
Carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment of dopamine as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the label-free detection of iron (III) ions and dopamine
Heterogeneous assembled nanocomplexes for ratiometric detection of highly reactive oxygen species in vitro and in vivo
Sensing metal ions with ion selectivity of a crown ether and fluorescence resonance energy transfer between carbon dots and graphene
Deep ultraviolet photoluminescence of water-soluble self-passivated graphene quantum dots
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Carbon nanoparticles as visible-light photocatalysts for efficient CO2 conversion and beyond
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Amphiphilic egg-derived carbon dots: rapid plasma fabrication, pyrolysis process, and multicolor printing patterns
An electrochemical avenue to blue luminescent nanocrystals from multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs)
Electrochemiluminescence of water-soluble carbon nanocrystals released electrochemically from graphite
One-step ultrasonic synthesis of water-soluble carbon nanoparticles with excellent photoluminescent properties
Highly luminescent carbon nanodots by microwave-assisted pyrolysis
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot
Oxygen-driven, high-efficiency production of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from alkanolamines and their application for two-photon cellular imaging
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
Bifunctional fluorescent carbon nanodots: green synthesis via soy milk and application as metal-free electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction

Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low-cost, green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu(II) ions
Easy synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from gelatin and their luminescent properties and applications
Green and size-controllable synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanoparticles from waste plastic bags
Highly luminescent N-doped carbon quantum dots as an effective multifunctional fluorescence sensing platform
Low-temperature synthesis of high-purity single-walled carbon nanotubes from alcohol
Gram-scale production of graphene based on solvothermal synthesis and sonication
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: electrochemical synthesis and their pH sensitive photoluminescence properties
Nanosized carbon particles from natural gas soot
